summary: Atwood uses the famous ceramics class parable from 'Art & Fear' to argue that producing large quantities of work leads to higher quality outcomes than obsessing over perfection. The ceramics students graded on quantity ended up creating better pots than those graded on quality, because they learned through iteration while the perfectionists merely theorized. He draws a direct parallel to software development: building many projects teaches you more than agonizing over building the perfect one. This applies equally to blogging, where committing to a regular schedule matters more than waiting for the perfect post.
key_points: The ceramics class experiment showed that students graded on quantity produced higher quality work than those graded on quality
Perfectionists spent time theorizing while practitioners learned through doing and failing
The principle applies across domains: software development, blogging, and any creative craft
Mental commitment to repeated practice is the prerequisite for improvement
Building the wrong thing still teaches you more than endlessly planning the right thing
money_quotes: The works of highest quality were all produced by the group being graded for quantity.

While the 'quantity' group was busily churning out piles of work – and learning from their mistakes – the 'quality' group had sat theorizing about perfection, and in the end had little more to show for their efforts than grandiose theories and a pile of dead clay.
Until you've mentally committed to doing it over and over, you will not improve. You can't.
If you aren't building, you aren't learning.
Rather than agonizing over whether you're building the right thing, just build it.
key_insight: Iteration through quantity is the path to quality—you cannot improve without the mental commitment to build repeatedly and learn from your mistakes.
